1: Timšōk liwyātān bĕḥakkāh: 'Can you pull up Leviathan with a hook?' — liwyātān (Leviathan): the great sea-serpent/dragon. The name likely derives from lāwāh (to coil/twine) + the augmented form. In Ugaritic mythology, Leviathan (ltn) is the seven-headed sea-dragon defeated by Baal. The OT uses the figure variably: as a literal creature (here and Ps 104:26) and as a mythological chaos-symbol (Isa 27:1; Ps 74:14). God's ownership of Leviathan is the theological point. Ûḇĕḥeḇel tišqîaʿ lĕšônô: 'or hold down his tongue with a rope?' — capture is impossible.
2: Hăḵĕrōm tāšîm bĕʾappô: 'Can you put a rush through his nose?' — ʾagmôn (rush, reed): the material for a nose-ring. The nose-ring/hook was the standard device for controlling large animals. Ûḇĕḥôaḥ tiṭqoḇ laḥyô: 'or run a hook through his jaw?' — the jaw-hook: equally impossible. Neither instrument of animal mastery works on Leviathan.
3: Hăyarbeh ʾēlêkā taḥănûnîm: 'Will he beg you for mercy?' — taḥănûnîm (pleas for favor/mercy): the petitioner's vocabulary. The absurdity: Leviathan as suppliant before a human. Ûyĕdabbēr ʾēlêkā rakkôt: 'will he address you with soft words?' — rāk (soft, gentle): the opposite of the harsh words he actually produces. Leviathan does not plead; he threatens.
4: Hăyikrōt bĕrît ʾittāk: 'Will he make a covenant with you?' — kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant): the covenant-making formula. Leviathan cannot be treaty-bound. Tiqāḥennû leʿeḇed ʿôlām: 'will you bind him as your permanent slave?' — leʿeḇed ʿôlām (permanent slave): the deepest category of servitude. Impossible with Leviathan.
5: Hătĕśaḥeq-bô kĕṣippôr: 'Will you play with him like a bird?' — the diminutive comparison: a pet bird vs. Leviathan. Ûtiqqĕšennû lĕnaʿărôtêkā: 'will you leash him for your young women?' — the domestication-play imagery. The absurdity drives home the point: no human treats Leviathan as a pet.
6: Yikrû ʿālāyw ḥabbārîm: 'Will trading partners negotiate for him?' — ḥabbār (partner, associate): the commercial-partner term. Leviathan cannot be commodified or traded. Yĕḥaṣṣûhû bên kĕnaʿănîm: 'will merchants distribute him among buyers?' — the Phoenician trading network (kĕnaʿĕnîm = Canaanites = traders) cannot handle Leviathan as merchandise.
7: Hătĕmallēʾ bĕśukkôt ʿôrô: 'Can you fill his skin with harpoons?' — sukkāh (here: harpoon/barbed spear — specialized fishing equipment): the instruments of large-game marine hunting. ûḇĕṣaltĕʾselê dāgāh rōʾšô: 'or his head with fishing lances?' — the futility of applying normal hunting tools to Leviathan.
8: Śîm-ʿālāyw kappekā: 'Lay your hand on him' — the imperative of attempted contact. Tizkōr milḥāmāh ʾal-tôsep: 'you will remember the encounter and never try again' — a single touch will be sufficient to teach permanent respect. The experiential knowledge of Leviathan cannot be acquired a second time.
9: Hēn tôḥaltô niḵzāḇāh: 'Any hope of mastering him is an illusion' — niḵzāḇāh (is proved false/deceptive): the hope of subduing Leviathan is inherently false hope. ʾAp-ʾel-marʾāyô yulāl: 'even the sight of him knocks a man down' — not the encounter but merely the sight produces collapse. Leviathan overwhelms by appearance alone.
10: Lōʾ-ʾakzār kî yĕʿûrennû: 'No one is fierce enough to rouse him' — ʾakzār (fierce, ruthless): the quality required to provoke Leviathan. ûmî hûʾ lĕpānay yitʿaṣṣāḇ: 'who then is able to stand before me?' — the pivot: if no one dares rouse Leviathan, who dares stand before the Creator of Leviathan? The rhetorical question moves from creature to Creator.
11: Mî hiqdîm-lî waʾăšallēm: 'Who has given to me first, that I should owe him in return?' — hiqḏîm (to anticipate, go before, give first): the claim that God owes anything to a creature because the creature acted first. Paul quotes this in Rom 11:35. Taḥat kol-haššāmayim lî-hûʾ: 'everything under all the heavens belongs to me' — the absolute sovereignty statement that makes obligation to a creature logically impossible. No creature can place God in its debt.
12: Lōʾ-ʾaḥărîš baḏāyw: 'I will not be silent about his structure/limbs' — baḏ (limb, part, branch): Leviathan's anatomical components will be narrated. ûḏbar gĕḇûrôt ûḥîn ʿerkô: 'his power and the grace of his proportion' — ḥîn ʿerkô (the grace/beauty of his arrangement): Leviathan is described as beautiful. The chaos-creature has aesthetic excellence.
13: Mî-gillāh pĕnê lĕḇûšô: 'Who can remove the outer layer of his skin?' — lĕḇûš (garment, outer layer): the skin as clothing. Bĕḵepal rissĕnô mî yāḇôʾ: 'who can break through his double layer of defense?' — riśsôn (bridle, bit — here: the double armor): the overlapping armor-like skin of the crocodilian.
14: Daltê pānāyw mî pātāḥ: 'Who can pry open the doors of his mouth?' — the jaws as locked doors. Sĕḇîḇôt šinnāyw ʾêmāh: 'his teeth are ringed with dread' — šinnāyw (teeth): surrounding each tooth is ʾêmāh (dread, terror): the teeth themselves inspire terror.
15: Gāʾăwāh ʾăpîqê māginnîm: 'His back is like rows of shields' — the scale-armor described as military shields in formation. Sāgûr ḥôtām ṣar: 'locked together like a tight seal' — ḥôtām (seal, signet): the scales fit as tightly as a seal impression, leaving no gap.
16: ʾEḥāḏ bĕʾeḥāḏ yiggāšû: 'Each one pressed so close to the next' — the interlocking precision of the scales. Wĕrûaḥ lōʾ-yāḇôʾ bênêhem: 'no breath of air passes between them' — the absolute seal of Leviathan's armor: not even air can penetrate. The most complete defensive structure in creation.
17: ʾîš bĕʾāḥîw yĕdubāqû: 'They cling together one to another' — the scales described as companions clinging. Yitlapĕtû wĕlōʾ yitpārāḏû: 'they lock onto each other and will not separate' — lāpaṯ (to grasp, seize): the scales' mutual grip is permanent.
18: ʿAṭîšōtāyw ʾôr tĕhallel: 'His sneezes flash light' — the dragon's fire-breathing described through the sneeze. Wĕʿênāyw kĕʿapʿappê šāḥar: 'his eyes shine like the gleam of dawn' — šāḥar (dawn): the eyelids of dawn as the comparison for Leviathan's eyes. Both fire and light: the creature embodies elemental power aesthetically.
19: Millĕpîw lappîḏîm yahalōkû: 'Burning torches go from his mouth' — the fire-breathing imagery. Kîḏôḏê ʾēš yimlāṭû: 'sparks of fire fly out' — kîḏôḏ (spark): the sparks of Leviathan's breath. The chaos-creature as a fire-elemental.
20: Minnĕḥîrāyw yēṣēʾ ʿāšān: 'Smoke billows from his nostrils' — the nostrils as smoke-vents. Kĕdûḏ nāpûaḥ wĕʾagmôn: 'like a boiling pot over burning rushes' — the domestic cooking-fire comparison: the world's most fearsome creature compared to a kitchen pot, but the quantity and heat are Leviathan's.
21: Napšô geḥālîm tĕlahaṭ: 'His breath sets coals afire' — nepeš (breath, soul, life): the breath as the source of fire. Wĕlahab mippiyw yēṣēʾ: 'a flame shoots out of his mouth' — the comprehensive fire-generation: both breath-heat and visible flame. The chaos-creature as lord of fire.
22: Bĕṣawwāʾrô yālîn ʿōz: 'Strength resides in his neck' — the neck as the seat of power. Wĕlĕpānāyw tādûṣ dĕʾābāh: 'dismay dances before him' — dĕʾābāh (dismay, sorrow, despair) as Leviathan's advance-companion. Terror precedes his movement.
23: Mapĕlê bĕśārô ḏāḇĕqû: 'The layers of his flesh are locked together' — dāḇēq (to cling, stick): the flesh as armored as the scales. Yāṣûq ʿālāyw bal-yimmôṭ: 'hard on him and immovable' — cast (yāṣaq) solid, not movable. The anatomical description emphasizes the invulnerability of every layer.
24: Libbô yāṣûq kĕmo-ʾāben: 'His heart is like cast stone' — the heart as the deepest/innermost layer: also cast solid. Wĕyāṣûq kĕpeleḥ taḥtît: 'as hard as the lower millstone' — the lower millstone (the fixed stone, not the rotating one): the hardest, most fixed stone in common experience. Leviathan's heart is the most interior expression of his invulnerability.
25: Mĕśāw yāgûrû ʾêlîm: 'When he rears up, the mighty are terrified' — ʾêl (strong man, mighty one): even the powerful fear Leviathan's rising. Miššĕḇārîm yiṯḥaṭṭāʾû: 'they fall back before his thrashing' — šāḇar (waves, or here: thrashings): the waves/chaos-motions of Leviathan cause retreat.
26: Massîgēhû ḥereḇ bal-tāqûm: 'The sword that reaches him cannot hold' — every weapon fails against Leviathan's armor. Ḥănît wĕdarda ûširyāh: 'spear, dart, and javelin' — the full inventory of ancient ranged weapons: all useless.
27: Yaḥšōḇ lĕṯeḇen barzel: 'He regards iron as straw' — the invulnerability scale: iron is straw, bronze is rotten wood. Wĕnĕḥūšāh kĕʿēṣ rāqāḇ: 'bronze is like rotten wood' — the materials of weapon-making are worthless against Leviathan. The standard military materials fail completely.
28: Lōʾ-yabriḥennû ben-qāšeṯ: 'The arrow cannot make him flee' — ben-qāšeṯ (son of the bow/arrow): poetic for the arrow. Lĕqaš nehpĕḵû-lô ʾabnê-qālaʿ: 'sling stones become chaff to him' — sling stones, which could kill Goliath (1 Sam 17:49), are meaningless against Leviathan.
29: Kĕqaš neḥšĕḇû tôṭāḥ: 'He regards war clubs as straw' — the close-combat weapons are as useless as the ranged ones. Wayiśḥaq lĕraaš kîḏôn: 'he laughs at the shaking of a lance' — śāḥaq (to laugh): the laughter motif appears again, now applied to Leviathan laughing at human weapons, as the horse laughed at fear (39:22).
30: Taḥtāyw ḥaddûḏê ḥereś: 'His underbelly is like sharp pottery shards' — the only potentially vulnerable surface (the belly) is itself sharpened. Yirpaḏ ḥārûṣ ʿălê-ṭîṭ: 'he plows jagged furrows through the mud' — Leviathan's passage itself creates destruction. His belly weapon-izes the ground.
31: Yaṯrîaḥ kaṣṣîr mĕṣûlāh: 'He makes the depths churn like a boiling pot' — mĕṣûlāh (depth, abyss): the deep sea becomes a boiling pot under Leviathan's movement. Wĕyāśem kayĕrôqāḥ: 'he churns the sea like a jar of perfume' — the sea reduced to a stirred cosmetic jar. Leviathan's power expressed through domestic compression.
32: ʾAḥărāyw yaʾîr nātîḇ: 'A glistening path shines behind him' — the phosphorescent wake of Leviathan's movement. Yaḥšōḇ tĕhôm lĕśêḇāh: 'one would think the sea had turned white' — the abyss (tĕhôm, the deep of Gen 1:2) appears white-haired behind Leviathan's wake. The deep itself appears to age in his presence.
33: ʾên ʿălāyw mōšēl: 'There is nothing on earth to compare with him' — māšal (to rule, to compare): no earthly creature masters Leviathan or serves as his comparison. ʿĀśûy lĕḇĕlî-ḥāt: 'he is fashioned without fear' — bĕlî-ḥāt (without fear): fearlessness built into his design. The creature who inspires fear in everything else was made without fear himself.
34: Kol-gāḇōah yirʾeh: 'He surveys everything that exalts itself' — Leviathan as the surveyor-king of the proud. Hûʾ melek ʿal-kol-bĕnê-šāḥaṣ: 'he is king over all the proud' — bĕnê šāḥaṣ (children of pride/arrogance): the proud and haughty are Leviathan's subjects. The chaos-monster who cannot be tamed by any human king is himself king of everything that exalts itself. The implication: Job's proud demand for a legal confrontation with God placed him in Leviathan's realm. The God who made and masters Leviathan is the one Job was challenging.
